title: Liberalism Must Get Radical
gist: Fareed Zakaria, drawing on Adrian Wooldridge's book 'The Revolutionary Center,' argues establishment liberalism is losing ground to democratic socialists (Mamdani-aligned NYC primary winners, UK's Andy Burnham) and right-wing populists because it became the ideology of entrenched institutional power rather than a challenger to it, and prescribes a return to liberalism's original anti-monopoly, anti-privilege radicalism.

insights.claim: Establishment liberalism is losing political ground because it became the ideology of the establishment it once existed to attack.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Frames the core investable political-risk thesis of the piece: expect continued erosion of establishment-liberal parties' vote share to both socialist and populist insurgents in the US and UK.
insights.quote: It became the ideology of the establishment it once existed to attack. That’s why it’s losing.

insights.claim: Andy Burnham, tipped to replace Keir Starmer as UK prime minister, campaigns on 'business-friendly socialism,' while New York's June 2026 Democratic primaries produced wins for democratic socialists — evidence the insurgent left is converting street protest into institutional power on both sides of the Atlantic.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: A concrete leading indicator — Burnham's rise as Starmer's likely successor signals UK Labour's center of gravity may be shifting leftward, relevant for UK policy and market positioning.
insights.locator: In Britain, the man widely expected to replace

insights.claim: The socialist trend is not uniform: moderate Democrats won most primaries outside New York City, including combat veteran Cait Conley in a swing district just outside the city.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: A necessary caveat against overreading Mamdani-style wins as a national Democratic-party-wide socialist shift — moderates remain competitive outside deep-blue urban primaries.

insights.claim: Citing Adrian Wooldridge's book 'The Revolutionary Center,' Zakaria argues liberalism was originally the most radical force in politics — a battering ram against inherited privilege, monopoly power, censorship, aristocracy, clerical authority, and closed guilds — not the ideology of the establishment it has since become.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Sets up the article's normative benchmark: liberalism's legitimacy problem is measured against its own founding anti-monopoly, anti-privilege identity.
insights.quote: It was not the ideology of the establishment. It was the battering ram against the establishment.

themes.theme: Two failures: passivity and the new aristocracy

insights.claim: Modern liberalism's 'passivity' failure — deregulating both economic and personal life since the 1990s while treating the consequences as the price of freedom — let corporate consolidation and inequality run unchecked and produced 'liberal fatalism,' e.g. blaming homelessness on housing scarcity and obesity on 'food deserts' rather than confronting the processed-food companies that hook consumers.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Identifies specific policy blind spots (homelessness framing, food-industry accountability, social-media attention capture) where liberal politics has avoided confronting corporate power — a durable line of political attack from both left and right.
insights.quote: liberals are more comfortable blaming “food deserts” than taking on the companies that hook their customers on processed food.

insights.claim: Wooldridge calls for reviving 'liberal paternalism': a liberal society should celebrate individual rights while also demanding individual responsibility, recognizing that freedom can be destroyed by addiction, monopoly, crime, ignorance, and dependence, not only by an overreaching state.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: A reusable policy framework distinct from both laissez-faire liberalism and socialism — worth tracking as a potential centrist policy platform differentiator in 2026-2028 elections.
insights.quote: a liberal society should celebrate individual rights—and also demand individual responsibility.
insights.locator: Wooldridge calls for a revival of liberal paternalism

insights.claim: Liberalism's second failure is that its meritocratic elite has hardened into 'its own aristocracy': elite liberals support social-justice rhetoric while resisting legacy-admissions reform and new housing construction in their own affluent neighborhoods, and have built a diversity bureaucracy that often judges people by group identity rather than individual character.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Names concrete elite self-interest mechanisms (legacy admissions, exclusionary zoning, DEI bureaucracy) that under investor/policy tracking act as durable political liabilities for liberal-coded institutions and jurisdictions.
insights.quote: the meritocratic elite has hardened into its own aristocracy.

insights.claim: K-12 education is the clearest example of liberal institutional capture: genuine reform would require attacking any union, bureaucracy, school board, or university department that protects its own power at the expense of children's outcomes.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Flags K-12 governance/union reform as a specific policy battleground where 'true liberal' credibility will be tested — relevant to education-policy and municipal-bond-adjacent political risk assessments.
insights.quote: attack any institution—union, bureaucracy, school board, university department—that feeds its own power while failing America’s children.
insights.locator: Nowhere is this clearer than in K-12 education

themes.theme: Why populists and socialists are winning, and liberalism's fix

insights.claim: Democratic socialists and right-wing populists are gaining ground because they credibly posture as outsiders fighting entrenched privilege — even though their answers (class warfare, protectionism, and state control on the left; protectionism, ethnic resentment, and racial nostalgia on the right) are, in Zakaria's view, bad ones.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Explains the mechanism by which both left- and right-wing insurgents outcompete centrist liberals on message even with substantively different (and per the author, flawed) policy platforms — key for anticipating further insurgent-candidate momentum.
insights.quote: they sound like outsiders willing to take on entrenched privilege and offer protection in a world where freedom seems to mean chaos.
insights.locator: This is where democratic socialists and right-wing populists

insights.claim: Zakaria's prescription is not to abandon liberalism but to recover its radical spirit: liberals should target monopoly and inherited advantage, take on corporate power that crushes markets, government power that protects insiders, and cultural bureaucracies that substitute group identity for individual dignity.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: The article's closing forward-looking claim — liberalism's electoral survival is conditioned on re-adopting an explicitly anti-monopoly, anti-incumbent political identity rather than defending the status quo.
insights.quote: Liberalism began as a revolt against encrusted power. It will survive only if it becomes one again.
insights.locator: Liberalism began as a revolt against encrusted power
